content: He had successfully avoided meeting his landlady on the staircase.
And each time he passed, the young man had a sick, frightened feeling, which made him scowl and feel ashamed.
He was hopelessly in debt to his landlady, and was afraid of meeting her.
He had given up attending to matters of practical importance; he had lost all desire to do so.
Taking a new step, uttering a new word is what they fear most....
It's simply a fantasy to amuse myself; a plaything!
Yes, maybe it is a plaything."
Trifles, trifles are what matter!
Why, it's just such trifles that always ruin everything...."
He had counted them once when he had been lost in dreams.
The young man stepped into the dark entry, which was partitioned off from the tiny kitchen.
The old woman stood facing him in silence and looking inquiringly at him.
The old woman coughed and groaned at every instant.
I am again on the same errand," Raskolnikov continued, a little disconcerted and surprised at the old woman's mistrust.
The old woman paused, as though hesitating; then stepped on one side, and pointing to the door of the room, she said, letting her visitor pass in front of her:
"Step in, my good sir."
But there was nothing special in the room.
In the corner a light was burning before a small ikon.
There was not a speck of dust to be seen in the whole flat.
These two rooms made up the whole flat.
"But the time is up for your last pledge.
"Give me four roubles for it, I shall redeem it, it was my father's.
I shall be getting some money soon."
"A rouble and a half, and interest in advance, if you like!"
"A rouble and a half!" cried the young man.
"Please yourself"--and the old woman handed him back the watch.
"Hand it over," he said roughly.
The old woman fumbled in her pocket for her keys, and disappeared behind the curtain into the other room.
The young man, left standing alone in the middle of the room, listened inquisitively, thinking.
"It must be the top drawer," he reflected.
"So she carries the keys in a pocket on the right.
Strong boxes always have keys like that... but how degrading it all is."
The old woman came back.
"Just so."
The young man did not dispute it and took the money.
You are too quick....
and can I, can I possibly....
He walked along the pavement like a drunken man, regardless of the passers by, and jostling against them, and only came to his senses when he was in the next street.
Without stopping to think, Raskolnikov went down the steps at once.
"All that's nonsense," he said hopefully, "and there is nothing in it all to worry about!
It's simply physical derangement.
Their departure left the room quiet and rather empty.
There was another man in the room who looked somewhat like a retired government clerk.
Suppose he gets me lessons, suppose he shares his last farthing with me, if he has any farthings, so that I could get some boots and make myself tidy enough to give lessons... hm...
That's not what I want now.
"Could I have expected to set it all straight and to find a way out by means of Razumihin alone?" he asked himself in perplexity.
"I shall go to Razumihin's of course, but... not now.
The flowers especially caught his attention; he gazed at them longer than at anything.
His legs felt suddenly heavy and a great drowsiness came upon him.
He turned homewards, but reaching Petrovsky Ostrov he stopped completely exhausted, turned off the road into the bushes, sank down upon the grass and instantly fell asleep.
Drunken and horrible looking figures were hanging about the tavern.
"I'll take you all, get in!"
"Why, Mikolka, are you crazy to put a nag like that in such a cart?"
"Get in, I'll take you all," Mikolka shouted again, leaping first into the cart, seizing the reins and standing straight up in front.
"Get in!
Come along!"
"All right!
Give it to her!"
Six men got in and there was still room for more.
That wretched nag was to drag all the cartload of them at a gallop!
"Come along, come along!" said his father.
I'll do for her!"
"Did anyone ever see the like?
A wretched nag like that pulling such a cartload," said another.
"You'll kill her," shouted the third.
"Don't meddle!
I will have her go at a gallop!..."
Even the old man could not help smiling.
The woman went on cracking nuts and laughing.
"I'll teach you to kick," Mikolka shouted ferociously.
"He'll kill her!"
Why have you stopped?" shouted voices in the crowd.
She sank back on her haunches, but lurched forward and tugged forward with all her force, tugged first on one side and then on the other, trying to move the cart.
Mikolka was in a fury that he could not kill her at one blow.
"Finish her off," shouted Mikolka and he leapt beside himself, out of the cart.
Mikolka stood on one side and began dealing random blows with the crowbar.
He stood as though regretting that he had nothing more to beat.
At that instant his father, who had been running after him, snatched him up and carried him out of the crowd.
Let us go home," he said to him.
"Father!
Why did they... kill... the poor horse!" he sobbed, but his voice broke and the words came in shrieks from his panting chest.
"They are drunk....
He tried to draw a breath, to cry out-and woke up.
He waked up, gasping for breath, his hair soaked with perspiration, and stood up in terror.
"But what is it?
Good God, can it be?"
My God!
Why, why then am I still...?"
He was pale, his eyes glowed, he was exhausted in every limb, but he seemed suddenly to breathe more easily.
It was as though an abscess that had been forming for a month past in his heart had suddenly broken.
Freedom, freedom!
He was free from that spell, that sorcery, that obsession!
It was about nine o'clock when he crossed the Hay Market.
Raskolnikov particularly liked this place and the neighbouring alleys, when he wandered aimlessly in the streets.
He already knew all about Lizaveta and she knew him a little too.
She was a complete slave and went in fear and trembling of her sister, who made her work day and night, and even beat her.
They were talking of something with special warmth.
"Come round to morrow about seven.
They will be here too."
"To morrow?" said Lizaveta slowly and thoughtfully, as though unable to make up her mind.
"Am I to come?"
"About seven o'clock to morrow.
You will be able to decide for yourself."
"And we'll have a cup of tea," added his wife.
"All right, I'll come," said Lizaveta, still pondering, and she began slowly moving away.
Raskolnikov had just passed and heard no more.
His first amazement was followed by a thrill of horror, like a shiver running down his spine.
He was only a few steps from his lodging.
